figures.caption: Figure 3: StyleGAN mapper [Karras et al., 2019] (left) vs. our RTM (right): a shared block iterated L times (blue) repeated for H refinement steps (red). The IMLE loss is applied only to the final style w; no supervision is applied between refinement steps.
Figure 12: RS-IMLE+RTM neighbours more faithfully match the query across gender, skin tone, age, and hair attributes that the baseline often fails to preserve.
Table 5: FID on nine few-shot benchmarks (256×256, 5,000 samples). Bold: best per row.
Figure 8: Random samples from RS-IMLE + RTM on Anime.
Figure 15: Unconditional CelebA-HQ 256×256 samples from RS-IMLE + RTM.

Table 1: Unconditional CIFAR-10 (32×32, 50,000 samples). Time / image is the wall-clock per generated image at batched inference on a single H100 (lower is faster); see App. H for the protocol. Bold/underline: best/second-best per metric column. † taken from the original publication.
Figure 9: SLERP interpolations in latent space from RS-IMLE + RTM on Shells and Dog.
Figure 4: Decoder architectures used across our RS-IMLE experiments.

Figure 2: Each circle marks one of quality, diversity, or fast (1-step) sampling; families sit at the intersections. RTM-IMLE pushes IMLE into the triple intersection. Adapted from Xiao et al. [2022].
Figure 18: AFHQ-v1 with StyleGAN2-ADA. Top: baseline. Bottom: with our RTM mapper. Second set of examples.
Figure 5: Random samples from RS-IMLE + RTM on Shells.
Figure 10: SLERP interpolations in latent space from RS-IMLE + RTM on Cat and Anime.

Figure 14: CelebA-HQ 256×256 comparison between RS-IMLE baseline and RS-IMLE + RTM. Top: left is without RTM, right is with RTM. Bottom: top rows are without RTM, bottom rows are with RTM. RTM generates sharper images with greater variety in age, skin tone, and expression, consistent with the improved Precision and Recall in Table 2.
Table 3: RTM as the StyleGAN2 / StyleGAN2-ADA mapper. Each (no RTM, +RTM) pair shares the same training pipeline; only the mapping network changes. Bold marks the better entry in each pair.

Table 2: Unconditional CelebA-HQ (256×256, 30,000 samples). Time / image as in Table 1 (single H100, batched). Bold/underline: best/second-best per metric column. Rows without † are our evaluations of each method’s released checkpoint. RS-IMLE + RTM uses the (H=16, L=2) configuration.
Figure 6: Random samples from RS-IMLE + RTM on Dog.
Figure 17: AFHQ-v1 with StyleGAN2-ADA. Top: baseline. Bottom: with our RTM mapper. First set of examples.
Figure 13: RS-IMLE+RTM neighbours better cluster around the query’s class and visual characteristics, reflecting improved mode coverage over the baseline.
Figure 11: ∼4,000 unconditional CIFAR-10 samples from our RS-IMLE + RTM, (H=16, L=1). Best viewed zoomed in.
Table 4: Effect of varying the number of refinement steps H at inference for the trained (H=16, L=1) configuration. Bold: best per dataset.
Figure 16: Unconditional CelebA-HQ 256×256 samples from RS-IMLE + RTM.
Figure 7: Random samples from RS-IMLE + RTM on Cat.
Table 6: Precision and Recall on the nine few-shot benchmarks (256× 256, 1,000 samples). Bold: best per metric.
